channel.title: Germany on Share Hub
channel.description: Recent content in Germany on Share Hub

item.title: Social Life and Making Friends: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Social Life & Making Friends: Turkey vs Germany
#

“Germans are cold.” Is it true? It’s more complicated than that.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Warm, Fast, and Deep
#

Turkish social culture is famously warm. Strangers become friends quickly. Hospitality is almost a competitive sport — guests must be fed, tea must be offered, no one leaves without feeling welcomed.

item.title: Housing and Renting: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Housing & Renting: Turkey vs Germany
#

Finding a flat in Munich is one of the hardest things I’ve done. Here’s the full picture.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Flexible but Volatile
#

Renting in Turkey is relatively straightforward in process — you find a place, negotiate with the landlord, sign a contract, move in. Personal relationships matter: landlords often prefer tenants they know or who come recommended.

item.title: Food and Eating Out: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Food & Eating Out: Turkey vs Germany
#

Turkish cuisine is world-famous for a reason. But German food culture has surprises of its own.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Food as Love Language
#

In Turkey, food is central to everything — family, hospitality, identity, celebration. Turkish cuisine is genuinely one of the richest in the world: fresh ingredients, regional variety, generations of technique passed down through families.

item.title: Shopping and Daily Errands: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Shopping & Daily Errands: Turkey vs Germany
#

Sunday everything is closed. Yes, really. Here’s how to adapt — and what you might actually prefer.

🇹🇷 Turkey: Open, Loud, and Always Available
#

Shopping in Turkey is a full sensory experience. Markets are open late, corner shops (bakkal) are open almost around the clock, and if you run out of something at 11pm, there’s almost always somewhere to go.

item.title: Health System: Turkey vs Germany
item.description:
Health System: Turkey vs Germany
#

From walking into any hospital to waiting weeks for a specialist — here’s what actually changed when I moved

Healthcare is one of those things you don’t think about until you need it. Moving countries means rebuilding your entire relationship with the medical system from scratch — new doctors, new insurance, new language, new rules.

item.title: Turkey vs Germany: An Honest Comparison After Making the Move
item.description:
Turkey vs Germany: An Honest Comparison After Making the Move
#

Not a travel guide. Not a rant. Just what I’ve actually experienced living in both countries.

I want to be clear upfront: this isn’t about which country is “better.” It’s about what I personally found different, harder, easier, worse, and surprisingly better — from both sides. Because Germany has real frustrations too.

item.title: Why I Left Turkey: The Decision That Changed Everything
item.description:
Why I Left Turkey: The Decision That Changed Everything
#

A personal account of weighing family, freedom, and the future — from someone who actually made the move

Moving to another country is never just a practical decision. It’s emotional, messy, and deeply personal. I’ve been asked many times: “Why did you leave?” This is my honest answer.

item.title: Trust as a Way of Life: What Germany Taught Me About Believing in People
item.description:
Trust as a Way of Life: What Germany Taught Me About Believing in People
#

Living in Munich as an expat from Turkey — small details that changed how I see society

Germany surprised me. Not with its punctuality, not with its efficiency — but with something quieter and harder to name: trust. The kind of trust that doesn’t ask for proof. The kind that assumes you’ll do the right thing, even when no one is watching.

item.title: Driving License Exchange in Germany | The Reality of Turkish & Bulgarian Licenses
item.description: Failing to plan the driving license exchange process early after moving to Germany can be costly. My experience trying to exchange a license via Bulgaria — and why it failed.

item.title: Switzerland Basel Travel Guide | One City, Three Countries: Switzerland, France, Germany (2026)
item.description: In Basel you can have breakfast in France in the morning and lunch in Germany. This city, standing at the exact intersection point of Switzerland, France and Germany, means three countries in a single trip.
